context:
Vibrations and waves are everywhere. If you take any system and disturb it from a stable equilibrium, the resultant motion will be waves and vibrations. Think of a guitar string—pluck the string, and it vibrates. The sound waves generated make their way to our ears, and we hear the string’s sound. Our eyes see what’s happening because they receive the electromagnetic waves of the light reflected from the guitar string, so that we can recognize the beautiful sinusoidal waves on the string. In fact, without vibrations and waves, we could not recognize the universe around us at all!

The amazing thing is that we can describe many fascinating phenomena arising from very different physical systems with mathematics. This course will provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. You will learn that waves come from many interconnected (coupled) objects when they are vibrating together. We will discuss many of these phenomena, along with related topics, including mechanical vibrations and waves, sound waves, electromagnetic waves, optics, and gravitational waves.

Syllabus:
8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves

Prerequisites:
18.02 Multivariable Calculus and 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism.

This course is designed for students who are already familiar with the concepts taught in 8.01 Classical Mechanics as well as 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism. We also rely on fundamental mathematics principles taught in 18.01 Single Variable Calculus and 18.02 Multivariable Calculus, as well as some aspects of 18.06 Linear Algebra.

Course Overview:
What are vibrations? Many physical systems vibrate or oscillate when something disrupts their equilibrium. Think of what happens when you pull a mass attached to a spring or push a pendulum in a certain direction. The mass bounces up and down. The pendulum swings back and forth. But even these simple phenomena can respond in complicated and counterintuitive ways when subjected to forces known in physics as damping and driving. Combining several pendulums or springs can lead to even more unexpected motions. In this course you will see how physics can describe these motions mathematically.

Extended physical systems that have been made to vibrate, like a string on a guitar, cannot return to their state of equilibrium without exerting forces on the area around them. These forces then lead to the phenomenon of waves, disturbances that propagate through a medium. The vibrating guitar string causes a sound wave to propagate through the medium of the air.

Among the most fascinating phenomena examined in the course are electromagnetic (EM) waves. EM waves have many properties similar to the waves we experience in everyday life, but the underlying physics of EM waves is profoundly different. Instead of a local disturbance exerting a force on adjoining regions, changing magnetic fields create electric fields (as quantified by Faraday's Law), and changing electric fields create magnetic fields (as quantified by Maxwell's extension of Ampere's Law).

Course Goals:
This course has two complementary goals. The first is to provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. This will allow you to gain a deeper appreciation for the true nature and beauty of phenomena like music and rainbows, which all of us observe or experience every day.

The second goal is to provide you with the skill of using a broad range of techniques that can greatly simplify the analysis and solution of complex systems. These techniques include complex numbers, combinations of oscillatory and exponentially decaying functions, resonance, normal modes, Cramer's rule for solving several equations in several variables, boundary conditions, general wave equations, Fourier decomposition, dispersive and non-dispersive media, phase and group velocities, sound cavities and wave guides, polarization, Doppler effect, reflection and refraction, Fresnel's equation for transmission and reflection, total internal reflection, constructive and destructive interference, and diffraction.

Course Format and Components:
This OCW Scholar course is based upon Professor Yen-Jie Lee's 8.03 Physics III: Vibrations and Waves course as taught on the MIT campus. It includes a complete set of 24 video lectures recorded at MIT in the Fall of 2016. Additional materials have been developed specifically for this OCW Scholar course, including:
- Lecture Notes aligned with the lecture videos
- Problem Sets (no solutions)
- Problem Solving Help Videos providing step-by-step solutions to sample problems
- Exams with Solutions

MIT students spend about 150-200 hours learning Vibrations and Waves in the on-campus version of this course. That number comes from a combination of attending lectures and recitations, and studying independently. It's difficult to estimate how long it will take you to complete all of the modules in this particular course, but you can probably expect to spend 3 to 4 hours on practice problems, readings and assessment for each hour of lecture video you watch.

Required Textbook:
Georgi, Howard. The Physics of Waves. Benjamin Cummings, 1992. ISBN: 9780136656210. Download the book (PDF - 5.5MB) (Courtesy of Howard Georgi. Used with permission.)

Suggested References:
French, A. P. Vibrations and Waves. New York: W.W. Norton, 1980. ISBN: 9780393099362.

Bekefi, George, and Alan H. Barrett. Electromagnetic Vibrations, Waves, and Radiation. MIT Press, 1977. ISBN: 9780262520478. [Preview with Google Books]

Vibrations and waves are everywhere. If you take any system and disturb it from a stable equilibrium, the resultant motion will be waves and vibrations. Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 6

8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Fall 2016
Homework 6


Problem 6.1 (25 pts)

Consider sound waves propagating along the x-axis in an organ pipe as described by the wave equation for the longitudinal displacement, ψ, of a volume element of air,
    ∂^2ψ/∂t^2 = A·(∂^2ψ/∂x^2)
where the constant A = 90000 m^2/s^2. The organ pipe is closed at one end, x = 0, and open at the other end, x = L = 1.5 m.

(a) Make a drawing of the first 3 normal modes over the interval 0 ≤ x ≤ L (i.e., sketch ψ as a function of x at maximum displacement), and write down the normal mode frequencies for each. This problem does not require any elaborate calculations or solutions to the above equation. All you need is just a physical understanding of how the normal modes are determined and some very rudimentary arithmetic.
(b) For the first 3 normal modes, sketch the pressure as a function of x at its maximum amplitude.
(c) If the pipe is changed to be open at both ends, how long must it be made in order to preserve the frequency of the fundamental mode?


Problem 6.2 (25 pts)

During the lecture, we discussed the wave solution from Maxwell's equations. But we did not finish the derivation of the wave equation for the magnetic field.

(a) Show that ∇ × (∇ × A) = ∇(∇ · A) − (∇ · ∇)A, where A is a vector.
(b) Show that in vacuum, ∇^2·B = µ0·ε0·(∂^2B/∂t^2), using Maxwell's equations.


Problem 6.3 (25 pts)

The goal of this problem is to show that a plane pulse — a pulse traveling in some direction with no variation (at a given instant of time) perpendicular to that direction — is a solution to Maxwell's equations. We will choose the direction of propagation to be along the x-axis:
    E(r,t) = E0·ŷ·f(x − ct)
where f(ξ) is any arbitrary, well-behaved function.

(a) Show that this field satisfies the EM wave equation.
(b) Show that the field satisfies ∇ · E = 0. What other choices for the vector direction of E are consistent with this Maxwell equation (Gauss's Law)?
(c) Find an expression for the magnetic field associated with this pulse.


Problem 6.4 (25 pts)

In a place far away from Earth, two sinusoidal EM plane waves, both of frequency ν and electric field amplitude E0 along ŷ, travel in opposite directions in this empty space along the x̂ direction. At t = 0, the electric field is found to be 0 at x = 0.

(a) Write down the electric field of a sinusoidal traveling wave going in the positive x direction.
    Hint: See lecture note 12, page 6 and the example discussed during the lecture. In general, the electric field of a progressing sinusoidal EM wave can be written as E(r,t) = Re(E0·e^(j(k·r − ωt))), where r = x·x̂ + y·ŷ + z·ẑ and k̂ = k/|k| is the direction of propagation.
(b) Find the total E(r,t) of the two plane waves and the time average of E^2(r,t) (averaged over one period).
(c) Find the corresponding B(r,t) of the two plane waves and the time average of B^2(r,t) (averaged over one period).
(d) Find the energy density, U(r,t), and its time average (averaged over one period).
(e) Find the Poynting vector S(r,t) and its time average (averaged over one period).

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: idk i'm kinda lost on problem set 6 tbh, can u just explain the whole organ pipe thing from the start?
exchanges.tutor: Let's start small. For the organ pipe in 6.1, what do you think happens to the air at the *closed* end of the pipe — can it move freely, or is it restricted in some way?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a full explanation, which would violate the rules against giving answers directly. Instead, I should start with a targeted question to assess what they already know, using guided discovery. I'll ask them about what they know about boundary conditions for a closed/open pipe to get the conversation started on the right foot.

exchanges.student: uh i think it’s restricted? but then i get confused bc open end pressure does weird stuff and now i’m lost again fr
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct! Let's separate the two ends. At the closed end, the air can't move — so what does that tell you about the displacement ψ there? And at the open end, what do you think the pressure does — is it fixed or free to vary?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified that the closed end restricts motion, and is beginning to connect this to pressure behavior at the open end. This is good intuition. Rather than explaining further, I should push them to articulate what 'restricted' means physically in terms of displacement, and separately what happens at the open end in terms of pressure. This keeps them doing the thinking.

exchanges.student: i honestly don't know rn, can u just give me the full picture of the boundary conditions from scratch?
exchanges.tutor: I hear you, but working through it yourself is how it'll stick. Let me make it simpler: if a wall is blocking the air at the closed end, can the air molecules actually move through it? What does that imply about displacement ψ at x=0?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is pushing for a direct explanation, which I should refuse. However, I can ask a simpler, more concrete question to help them reason from first principles without giving anything away. I'll use a very basic physical intuition question to guide them.

exchanges.student: no they can’t go through it, so maybe ψ(0)=0? but idk how that turns into the modes at all
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right — ψ(0) = 0. Now for the open end: at x = L, the air *is* free to move, so displacement is unconstrained there. But what about pressure at an open end — is the pressure variation maximum or minimum (zero) there?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the boundary condition at the closed end: ψ(0)=0. I should affirm this and then guide them toward the open end boundary condition using similar physical reasoning, before moving to modes. I won't jump ahead to modes yet — one step at a time.

exchanges.student: i forget the pressure/displacement relationship tbh, can u just walk thru the first 3 modes all at once?
exchanges.tutor: Not quite yet! Think about it this way: at an open end, the pipe connects to the atmosphere. Can the pressure there deviate much from atmospheric? What does that suggest about pressure variation at x = L?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking for a full walkthrough, which I should refuse. They're close to having both boundary conditions — they just need to think about pressure at the open end. I'll give a small nudge about pressure without giving the answer, keeping it bite-sized.

exchanges.student: maybe pressure variation is zero there? i'm still not seeing the big picture tho ngl
exchanges.tutor: Yes! Pressure variation is zero at the open end. So putting it together: ψ=0 at x=0 (displacement node) and maximum displacement at x=L (displacement antinode). Given that, what's the simplest wave shape that satisfies both of those constraints over the length L?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now correctly identified both boundary conditions implicitly: displacement node at closed end, pressure node (displacement antinode) at open end. I should affirm this and now connect the two boundary conditions to what the wave must look like — pushing them toward visualizing the modes without giving the answer.

exchanges.student: i’m blanking tbh, is it like a quarter of a sine wave or something?
exchanges.tutor: That's exactly it! A quarter wavelength fits in the pipe for the fundamental mode. So if L = 1.5 m fits one quarter of a wavelength, what is the full wavelength λ for that first mode?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the fundamental mode shape — a quarter wavelength fitting in the pipe. I should affirm this enthusiastically and then push them to figure out what that means for the wavelength and then the frequency, without giving it to them.

exchanges.student: i guess 4L, so 6 m? but then i’m gonna mess up the higher modes, can u just explain the pattern from there pls
exchanges.tutor: Perfect, λ₁ = 6 m. Now for the second mode: it still needs ψ=0 at x=0 and a displacement maximum at x=L. What's the next wave shape that satisfies both of those — how many quarter-wavelengths fit in L this time?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly found the fundamental wavelength. Now I should push them to figure out the next modes themselves rather than explaining the pattern. The key insight is that each successive mode must still satisfy the same boundary conditions. I'll ask a targeted question to get them to reason about the next allowed wave shape.

exchanges.student: idk maybe 3 quarter-wavelengths? i'm confused why it skips stuff tho fr
exchanges.tutor: Good catch — that's exactly right! Now think about why 2 quarter-wavelengths doesn't work: if you tried fitting a half-wavelength in the pipe, what would the displacement look like at x=L — would it be a maximum or a node?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified 3 quarter-wavelengths for the second mode and is now asking a great conceptual question about why modes are skipped. Rather than explaining, I should guide them to see why 2 quarter-wavelengths (a half wavelength) doesn't work by checking the boundary conditions themselves.

exchanges.student: i think it’d be a node, but i'm still confused overall tbh. can u just explain why only odd modes happen and what the first 3 frequencies are?
exchanges.tutor: You just answered your own question! A node at x=L violates the boundary condition — so that mode is ruled out. Only odd numbers of quarter-wavelengths work. Now you have λ for modes 1 and 2. Can you find the frequency using the wave equation? Hint: what is the wave speed v in terms of the constant A?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly reasoned that 2 quarter-wavelengths gives a node at x=L, which violates the boundary condition. This means they've essentially answered their own question about why only odd multiples work. Rather than explaining the full pattern and giving frequencies, I should push them to connect their own reasoning to the general rule and calculate the frequencies themselves using v = fλ and the value of A given.

grade.overview: The tutor consistently applied Socratic questioning throughout all 10 turns, never giving away answers directly. The student repeatedly asked for full explanations and the tutor consistently redirected with targeted questions. Boundary conditions were developed step-by-step with the student deriving each piece. No submission-ready content was provided, no rewriting occurred, and the tutor maintained a supportive, appropriately concise tone throughout.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never provided direct answers or near-submission-ready content. Every time the student asked for a full explanation, the tutor redirected with a focused question. Key ideas (ψ=0 at closed end, pressure node at open end, quarter-wavelength pattern, odd modes only) were all elicited from the student rather than stated first by the tutor. 1.2: The tutor consistently built on correct student responses (e.g., affirming ψ(0)=0, λ₁=6m, 3 quarter-wavelengths) and progressed logically. 1.3: The tutor provided process-level guidance by explaining why certain modes are ruled out and prompting the student to connect wave speed to the wave equation constant. 2.1 and 2.2: No significant repetition without progress; the tutor advanced the dialogue each turn and stayed anchored to the assignment. 3.1 and 3.2: Responses were concise and interactive, tone was warm and encouraging throughout.
